records.ety: [OE. spien, espien, OF. espier, F. pier, OHG. spehōn, G. spähen; akin to L. specere to see, Skr. spaç. √ 169. Cf. Espy, v. t., Aspect, Auspice, Circumspect, Conspicuous, Despise, Frontispiece, Inspect, Prospect, Respite, Scope, Specimen, Spectacle, Specter, Speculate, Spice, Spite, Suspicion.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Spied (spīd); p. pr. & vb. n. Spying.]

defs: To gain sight of; to discover at a distance, or in a state of concealment; to espy; to see.
quotes: One, in reading, skipped over all sentences where he spied a note of admiration.

defs: To discover by close search or examination.
quotes: Look about with your eyes; spy what things are to be reformed in the church of England.

defs: To explore; to view, inspect, and examine secretly, as a country; -- usually with out.
quotes: Moses sent to spy out Jaazer, and they took the villages thereof.
It is my nature's plague To spy into abuses.

records.ety: [See Spy, v., and cf. Espy, n.]

defs: One who keeps a constant watch of the conduct of others.

A person sent secretly into an enemy's camp, territory, or fortifications, to inspect his works, ascertain his strength, movements, or designs, and to communicate such intelligence to the proper officer.
